title: SEGMENT 3 — Lytic versus lysogenic: two lifestyles, one switch

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Lytic versus lysogenic: two lifestyles, one switch
Phages come in two basic flavors of behavior, and the distinction between them turns out to be central — both to phage biology and to the experiments that founded molecular biology.
The two lifestyles are lytic and lysogenic.
A lytic phage is a hit-and-run killer.

It infects a bacterium, hijacks the cellular machinery, makes hundreds or thousands of copies of itself inside the host, and then bursts the host cell open — lyses it — releasing the new progeny phages to find new hosts.
The cell dies.
The phage population grows.

From infection to lysis can be as fast as twenty or thirty minutes for a phage like T4 — call it a doubling time of half an hour with a burst size of a hundred or two hundred, and you can imagine how fast a lytic infection rips through a bacterial population.
T4, T7, MS2 — these are obligate lytic phages.
They have no other gear.
Infect, replicate, lyse, repeat.
A lysogenic phage, by contrast, has two gears.
It can do the same lytic thing — infect, replicate, lyse — but it has the option of doing something else: integrating its DNA into the host's chromosome and going quiet.

The phage genome inserts itself, sometimes at a specific site, and the host bacterium goes back to its normal life, dividing and replicating its own DNA, faithfully copying the integrated phage DNA along with everything else.
This integrated state is called a prophage, and a bacterium carrying a prophage is called a lysogen.
The phage is essentially a passenger.

It rides along through generations of bacterial division, doing nothing, contributing nothing, costing very little, until something triggers it — typically DNA damage, stress, UV light — at which point the prophage excises itself from the chromosome, switches into lytic mode, and now it's the same hit-and-run killer it could have been from the start.
The cell that was its quiet host suddenly becomes its slaughterhouse.
So the analogy I want you to hold: lytic equals kill the host immediately.
Lysogenic equals integrate quietly and wait.
A lytic phage is a wolf.
A lysogenic phage is a sleeper agent.
And critically, lysogenic phages — the ones that can integrate and wait — possess a molecular switch that decides, on infection, which behavior to follow.
We'll spend Segment 5 entirely on that switch, because it's one of the most important pieces of biology ever worked out.
The classic lysogenic phage, the one that taught us almost everything we know about gene regulation, is lambda — bacteriophage lambda — which infects E. coli.
Lambda has a beautifully balanced switch: when it infects, it can go lytic (with a probability that depends on conditions) or it can integrate as a prophage and wait.
The same genome.
Two completely different outcomes.

The reason it has been so studied is that lambda forced biologists to confront, in a small and tractable system, the basic question of how cells decide: how does one piece of DNA produce two different developmental outcomes, depending on signals?
Lambda was the test case where we learned how to think about that question.
And the answer turned out to involve transcription factors binding DNA in mutually exclusive states, which is the foundation of all developmental biology.
There's a third twist on the lifestyle question that's worth a footnote.
M13 and its filamentous relatives are neither classically lytic nor classically lysogenic.
They establish a chronic, non-lytic infection — they don't integrate, but they also don't lyse.
The infected cell keeps living, continuously extruding new phages into the medium.

We discussed this in the phage display lecture because it's exactly the property that makes M13 so convenient as a display vehicle: you can grow infected cultures indefinitely and harvest phage from the supernatant.
So the world of phage lifestyles isn't strictly binary.
Lytic, lysogenic, chronic — there's a small zoo of strategies.
But lytic-versus-lysogenic is the central axis, and it's the one to anchor your mental model around.
One more practical implication of the lytic-lysogenic distinction, because it'll be important when we get to therapy.
For phage therapy you generally want lytic phages.
Why?
Because the whole point of therapy is to kill the pathogen.
A lysogenic phage that integrates into the pathogen's chromosome and goes quiet hasn't done anything useful to the patient.

Worse, lysogenic phages have a habit of carrying additional genes that aren't part of their own replication machinery — what we call morons, just to make the literature confusing — and some of those passenger genes are toxins or virulence factors.

The classic, terrible example is the diphtheria toxin, which is encoded not on the Corynebacterium diphtheriae chromosome but on a lysogenic phage that infected an ancestor of the bacterium and has been carried along ever since.
The bacterium is dangerous because the phage is there.
So lysogenic phages can arm bacteria, can enhance pathogenicity.
For therapy, you screen lytic phages, you confirm they're obligately lytic, and you reject the lysogenic ones.
Keep that in mind for later.
Alright.
Lytic and lysogenic.
The two basic lifestyles.
The switch between them is one of the foundational stories in molecular biology, and we'll get to it.
But first, let's talk about how we discovered any of this — because the discovery story is genuinely strange.
